focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

The trauma room smelled wrong before the yard fully woke.

It was not dirty. It was wrong. The ordinary braid was there, the camphor from Paloma's sleeves, the basil she kept alive in clinic cups along the narrow sill, the bitter slick of disinfectant rising off the drain grate, the heat starting to wake in the metal cabinets while the yard wall still held back the sun. Under it sat the colder note of the pathology cooler, lid unlatched before its hour, meltwater sweating down the blue plastic sides onto the stainless counter.

Marisol shut the door with her hip and checked the wall lens above the handwash sink. The red dot held steady. Corridor surveillance was live, as usual, but the room camera was still pointed three degrees too high after last week's maintenance and caught more fluorescence than counter. She had left it that way on purpose. Maintenance would not open a ticket for a camera that only missed the lower right corner, where Paloma kept the sharps tray and now the cooler.

Paloma had changed into a clean scrub top with lemons printed near the hem, a gift from some niece she never stopped mentioning and never seemed to visit. The fabric was already damp between her shoulder blades. Her silvering braid had darkened with sweat at the nape. When she reached for the cooler latch, the tremor in her right hand made the metal chatter once against the lid before she got it still with the heel of her left palm.

"You can still stop me," she said.

Marisol had already gone over the timing so many times it had started to sound like prayer and then like the kind of lie people told themselves before an accident. Audit swabs on the southbound layover hit at 06:18. The assay printer in sanitation took four minutes to kick back a quarantine code if a live sa...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...station ever did.

Lidia was already restacking the shelves, putting the room back into its ordinary disguise. "Don't leave on Mateo's heels," she said. "Station police notice when two frightened people use the same door and the same platform stairs. Count to sixty, then go with the next commuter spill."

Marisol stayed still for a second. In the station beyond the church wall, the nineteen-ten gave its departure chime, that clipped synthetic politeness the corridor used to make orders sound like service. She pictured the canvas pouch knocking against Mateo's leg as he boarded, the folder inside it rubbing against parish donation sheets and wedding banns and the paper proof of dead people once married under kinder bureaucracies. It wasn't enough for court, and it wasn't yet enough to tear the line open. It was enough to leave her empty-handed in the only way that helped.

When Marisol finally reached for her tote, it felt wrong, almost toy-light. She tucked the warm thermal receipt inside her bra, where the edge scratched at her damp skin with every breath. If Teresa stopped her now, she'd find bleach variances, a folded caution vest, and nothing she could claw back into company custody.

By the time Marisol stepped back into the alley, the nineteen-ten was already moving south to north, away from the concession office, carrying the pouch toward the city with the evening crowd and their groceries and their sweat and the ordinary loose change of after-work life. She waited until the last car cleared the viaduct, then turned back toward the yard. Quarter-close pickup would already be moving floor by floor. She needed to be in Teresa's line of sight again before absence became its own document. The white cars waited under their lights with rust coming through at the seams.
